questions.text: Agriculture is which type of economic activity?
questions.explanation: Primary activities include all those connected with extraction and production of natural resources. Agriculture, fishing and gathering are good examples.

questions.text: The word 'Agriculture' is derived from Latin words 'ager' or 'agri' meaning ______ and 'cultura' meaning ______.
questions.explanation: Agriculture comes from the Latin 'ager' (soil) and 'cultura' (cultivation).

questions.text: What percentage of the world's population is engaged in agricultural activity?
questions.explanation: Fifty per cent of persons in the world are engaged in agricultural activity.

questions.text: The science of commercial rearing of silk worms is known as:
questions.explanation: Sericulture is the commercial rearing of silk worms, which may supplement the income of the farmer.

questions.text: Viticulture refers to the cultivation of:
questions.explanation: Viticulture is specifically the cultivation of grapes.

questions.text: Breeding of fish in specially constructed tanks and ponds is called:
questions.explanation: Pisciculture is the breeding of fish in controlled environments like tanks or ponds.

questions.text: Growing vegetables, flowers, and fruits for commercial use is called:
questions.explanation: Horticulture involves the commercial production of fruits, vegetables, and flowers.

questions.text: Shifting cultivation is also known as ________ in North-East India.
questions.explanation: Shifting cultivation is known as Jhumming in North-East India, Milpa in Mexico, Roca in Brazil, and Ladang in Malaysia.

questions.text: In which type of farming are animals reared and crops grown on the same land?
questions.explanation: In mixed farming, the land is used for growing food and fodder crops and rearing livestock.

questions.text: Which of the following is a 'Plantation' crop?
questions.explanation: Plantations are a type of commercial farming where single crops of tea, coffee, sugarcane, cashew, rubber, banana or cotton are grown.

questions.text: Rice requires ______ temperature, ______ humidity and ______ rainfall.
questions.explanation: Rice is a tropical crop that needs high temperature, high humidity, and high rainfall to grow properly.

questions.text: Which soil is best suited for the growth of Wheat?
questions.explanation: Wheat thrives best in well-drained loamy soil.

questions.text: Which crop is also known as 'Coarse Grains'?
questions.explanation: Millets are known as coarse grains and can be grown on less fertile and sandy soils.

questions.text: Maize is also known as:
questions.explanation: Maize is commonly known as corn in many parts of the world.

questions.text: Cotton requires ______ frost-free days and bright sunshine for its growth.
questions.explanation: Cotton requires high temperature, light rainfall, 210 frost-free days and bright sunshine for its growth.

questions.text: Which crop is known as the 'Golden Fibre'?
questions.explanation: Jute is called the 'Golden Fibre' because of its color and value.

questions.text: Who is the leading producer of Coffee in the world?
questions.explanation: Brazil is the world's leading producer of coffee.

questions.text: Tea is a ______ crop grown on plantations.
questions.explanation: Tea is a beverage crop grown on large plantations, requiring cool climate and well-distributed high rainfall.

questions.text: Agricultural development refers to efforts made to increase farm production in order to meet the growing demand of increasing ________.
questions.explanation: The ultimate aim of agricultural development is to increase food security for the growing population.

questions.text: A farm in the USA is typically ________ than a farm in India.
questions.explanation: A typical farm size in the USA is about 250 hectares, compared to a small Indian farm of about 1.5 hectares.

questions.text: The input of a farm system includes:
questions.explanation: Seeds, fertilisers, machinery and labour are important inputs of the agricultural system.

questions.text: Which of these is a human input in agriculture?
questions.explanation: Storage, labour, machinery and chemicals are human inputs; rainfall and sunshine are physical inputs.

questions.text: Nomadic herding is practiced in the semi-arid and arid regions of:
questions.explanation: Nomadic herding is common in Sahara, Central Asia and parts of India like Rajasthan and Jammu & Kashmir.

questions.text: Commercial grain farming is mostly practiced in the sparsely populated grasslands of:
questions.explanation: Temperate grasslands of North America, Europe and Asia are major areas for commercial grain farming.

questions.text: Slash and burn agriculture is another name for:
questions.explanation: In shifting cultivation, a plot of land is cleared by felling trees and burning them, hence 'slash and burn'.

questions.text: In India, which crop is grown as a plantation crop in Assam?
questions.explanation: Tea in Assam and North Bengal is a major plantation crop.

questions.text: Major millets grown in India are:
questions.explanation: Jowar, bajra and ragi are the primary millets produced in India.

questions.text: Which country is the leading producer of Jute?
questions.explanation: India and Bangladesh are the world's leading producers of Jute.

questions.text: High yielding variety (HYV) seeds are a part of:
questions.explanation: HYV seeds, fertilisers and machinery are used to increase yield in modern agricultural development.

questions.text: Organic farming uses ________ instead of chemicals.
questions.explanation: In organic farming, organic manure and natural pesticides are used instead of chemicals; no genetic modification is done.

questions.text: Which of the following is a tertiary activity?
questions.explanation: Tertiary activities provide support to primary and secondary sectors through services like transport, trade, and banking.

questions.text: Commercial farming includes:
questions.explanation: All these types use crops and animals for sale in the market using large capital.

questions.text: Which region is known for 'Milpa' cultivation?
questions.explanation: Milpa is the name for shifting cultivation in Mexico.

questions.text: The thick wool of which animals is common in nomadic herding in Tibet?
questions.explanation: Yak and Sheep are common in Tibet and high altitude regions for wool and milk.

questions.text: Which fiber is also known as 'Synthetic fiber' (though not agricultural)?
questions.explanation: Nylon is synthetic; cotton, jute and silk are natural fibers.

questions.text: China is the leading producer of ______ in the world.
questions.explanation: China leads in rice production, followed by India, Japan, Sri Lanka and Egypt.

questions.text: Wheat is harvested in ______ in India.
questions.explanation: Wheat is grown in winter but harvested in summer (Rabi crop).

questions.text: Maize requires ______ temperature and ______ sunshine.
questions.explanation: Maize needs moderate temperature, rainfall and lots of sunshine.

questions.text: Which country uses heavy machinery and planes for agricultural work like pesticides?
questions.explanation: Large-scale commercial farming in the USA utilizes airplanes for sowing and spraying.

questions.text: The main objective of 'Food Security' is:
options: Making food available to all people at all times
questions.explanation: Food security exists when all people, at all times, have physical and economic access to sufficient, safe and nutritious food.
